content.assessment: No comparator country case in the evidence set documents a demographically-driven passive contraction of a selective track sector as a deliberate no-policy baseline, so there is no directly comparable international mechanism to benchmark against. The available comparators (Finland, Poland) involve active legislative interventions on tracking age, not passive demographic drift, meaning this proposal sits outside the tracking-reform comparator set entirely. Insofar as comparative evidence is informative here, it is by way of contrast: the reforms that produced measured inequality reductions (Finland) or that caused severe disruption when abruptly reversed (Poland) were both active, bundled, systemically designed interventions, not passive demographic outcomes—suggesting that absent active composition rules, cross-national evidence gives no basis to expect that demographic contraction alone would replicate the equity gains documented for deliberate structural reform. The geographically uneven urban/rural risk flagged in this proposal has no direct comparator test in the supplied set, limiting assessment further.
opportunities: Demographic contraction could in principle be paired with the comparator-informed composition rules used in active reforms, though this would move the proposal outside its own passive-baseline design
strengths: Avoids any risk of comparator-documented implementation shocks (e.g., Poland's 2017 double-cohort crisis) since no active structural transition is undertaken
threats: Absent active composition-focused rules, the SES/family-capital sorting mechanisms documented as central to Hungary's outsized between-school variance are not addressed by any comparator-tested mechanism, and passive contraction risks reproducing or worsening urban-rural selectivity disparities with no international precedent evaluating this specific risk pathway
weaknesses: No comparator country case in the evidence set models passive demographic contraction as an equity mechanism, so this lens has no basis for assessing comparability or transferability

The active, bundled nature of the reforms associated with measured inequality reduction (Finland) or measured disruption (Poland) suggests passive drift is a poor analogue to the mechanisms comparative evidence actually evaluates
